meta.title: X166 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes‑Benz GLS X166 engine — OM642, M276, M278, M157. Real UK costs, V8 cylinder scoring risks explained, and honest ownership advice. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz GLS X166 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X166 engine typically costs £3,200–£14,000 supply only, depending on engine — the volume OM642 diesel sits at £3,200–£5,500, while the AMG M157 V8 commands £9,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes‑Benz GLS X166 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 3.0‑litre V6 diesel is the most proven — it powered the X166 across its entire production run and has a documented reputation for longevity. The M276 V6 bi‑turbo petrol is the most reliable petrol choice, avoiding the M278 V8's cylinder scoring risks.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz GLS X166?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil cooler seal failure on the OM642 diesel, cylinder scoring on the M278 4.7‑litre V8, carbon buildup and oil consumption on the M157 AMG V8, and — unrelated to the engine — AIRMATIC suspension wear on high‑mileage examples.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz GLS X166 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. The OM642 diesel and M276 V6 petrol are solid choices. The M278 V8 is the riskiest — cylinder scoring is a well‑documented pattern that can appear at any mileage. The M157 AMG V8 is powerful but demands a maintenance budget to match its performance.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes‑Benz GLS X166 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M278 4.7‑litre V8 (GL 500 / GLS 500) is the one we consistently advise buyers to approach with extreme caution — cylinder scoring in the right cylinder head is a design‑level issue that can appear at almost any mileage and costs £6,000–£10,000 to fix.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my Mercedes‑Benz GLS X166 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil consumption or coolant contamination (OM642 oil cooler seals), misfire or oil consumption (M278 cylinder scoring), or major mechanical knock (any engine) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑Benz GLS X166 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the engine and the car's value. A £3,200–£5,500 OM642 recon against a £12,000–£25,000 X166 is 13–46% — safe. A £6,000–£10,000 M278 recon against a £12,000 example is 50–83% — borderline. Body condition and overall history are the deciding factors.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes‑Benz GLS X166 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 diesel can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing, though oil cooler seals are a recurring maintenance item. The M276 V6 petrol is similarly durable. The M278 V8 is more variable — some fail before 80,000 miles due to cylinder scoring, while others exceed 150,000 miles without issue.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the GL‑Class and GLS‑Class?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are the same X166 platform. The GL‑Class name was used pre‑facelift (2012–2015), and the GLS‑Class name was used post‑facelift (2015–2019) to align with the GLA, GLC, and GLE naming convention. The facelift also brought the 9G‑Tronic transmission and minor styling updates.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz GLS X166 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Tell us your variant (GL 350 CDI, GLS 400, GLS 63 AMG, etc.) and approximate registration date (pre‑ or post‑2015) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes‑Benz GLS X166 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X166 SUV — from the 3.0‑litre OM642 diesel to the 5.5‑litre M157 V8 in the AMG GLS 63. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the full‑size SUV ownership realities that define this generation. Backed by real UK X166 enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes‑Benz GLS X166 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X166 SUV specifically — spanning the GL‑Class (2012–2015) and GLS (2015–2019) eras.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM642: 90% confidence — well‑documented V6 diesel with oil cooler seal and swirl flap issues. M276: 85% confidence — proven V6 bi‑turbo with good reliability. M278: 70% confidence — known for cylinder scoring/heat‑related failures in right cylinder head. M157: 85% confidence — robust AMG V8 but with carbon buildup and oil consumption on higher‑mileage examples. All ratings are PROVISIONAL — pending dedicated Engine Pages for each family.

overview.intro: The X166 is the second‑generation full‑size luxury SUV from Mercedes‑Benz, launched at the 2012 New York International Auto Show as the GL‑Class and renamed GLS at the 2015 facelift. It replaced the X164 GL‑Class and positioned itself as the "S‑Class among SUVs" — a seven‑seat luxury flagship with air suspension, 4MATIC all‑wheel drive, and a range of V6 and V8 engines. A significant 2015 facelift brought the GLS name, 9G‑Tronic transmission (replacing the earlier 7G‑Tronic), and revised engine line‑up.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes‑Benz W164/W166 • Predecessor: X164 • Successor: X167 • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (standalone full‑size SUV).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The GLS/GL‑Class range (X166 combined) generated real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], with the OM642 diesel and AMG M157 V8 commanding the strongest specialist interest.

items.quote: The proven 3.0‑litre V6 turbo diesel that powered the X166 across its entire production run — 258hp, 620Nm of torque, and a documented reputation for longevity. Oil cooler seals are the main watchpoint, but the engine itself is robust and well‑supported.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want effortless V6 diesel torque without the M278's petrol‑engine risks.

items.quote: The facelift‑era V6 bi‑turbo delivers 367hp and 500Nm from a 3.0‑litre V6 — smooth, responsive, and without the cylinder scoring risks that plague the M278 V8. It's the sensible petrol choice in the X166 range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 petrol refinement without the M278 V8's reliability concerns.

items.quote: The 4.7‑litre V8 is the most contentious engine in the X166 range — cylinder scoring is a well‑documented pattern, particularly in the right cylinder head, caused by heat concentration in a thermally overloaded design. A full engine replacement is a five‑figure bill that can appear at almost any mileage.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who understand the risk and are prepared for a potential £6,000–£10,000 rebuild.

items.quote: The hand‑built AMG V8 delivers 577hp in facelift guise — and with it comes AMG‑level running costs. Carbon buildup, oil consumption, valve cover gasket leaks, and cooling system failures are all documented on higher‑mileage examples. A full recon is a five‑figure bill, and parts availability can be a headache.
items.whoItsFor: AMG enthusiasts with deep pockets and a trusted specialist.

items.quote: The V6 bi‑turbo offers genuine performance without the M278's risks or the M157's running costs — 333–367hp, 480–500Nm, and a proven track record across the Mercedes range. It's the sweet spot in the X166 petrol line‑up.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 performance without the V8's reliability concerns.

items.quote: The pre‑facelift OM642 diesel is often overlooked in favour of the GLS 350 d's 9G‑Tronic car — but the 7G‑Tronic GL 350 CDI offers the same 258hp and 620Nm, often at a lower purchase price, with the same robust diesel V6 underneath.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want X166 capability on a tighter budget.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Proven Diesel — robust but oil cooler‑sensitive
Best Petrol — more power, same costs
High Risk — cylinder scoring is the defining concern

economicsRule.title: The X166 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,200–£5,500 OM642 recon against a £12,000–£25,000 X166 sits at 13–46% of the car's value — well inside the 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold. Against a £10,000 high‑mileage example, the same recon reaches 32–55% — still viable, but a significant proportion of the car's residual value. However, a £6,000–£10,000 M278 recon against a £12,000 X166 is 50–83% — on a tired example, that's the borderline of the 70% threshold. Value the car and its engine history first.

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Failure
problems.affectedModels: X166 GL 350 CDI, GLS 350 d (all OM642.826).
problems.rootCause: The V6 diesel's oil cooler seals degrade over time, allowing oil to leak into the valley of the V — the labour to access the seals is significant, making this an expensive repair even though the parts are cheap.
problems.recommendation: A £800–£1,800 oil cooler seal replacement is dramatically cheaper than the £3,200–£5,500 replacement that follows oil starvation — address it proactively.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your X166 →

problems.title: M278 Cylinder Scoring
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 40,000–80,000 miles (but can occur at any mileage).
problems.rootCause: The 4.7‑litre V8 is thermally overloaded — heat concentration in the right cylinder head leads to cylinder scoring, which eventually causes loss of compression and misfire. This is not mileage‑dependent; it's a design flaw that can appear at any point.
problems.recommendation: On a £12,000–£25,000 X166, a £1,500–£3,000 bore scope inspection is essential before purchase — it protects you from the £6,000–£10,000 rebuild that follows cylinder scoring.

cta.label: Compare M278 engine prices for your X166 →

problems.title: M157 Carbon Buildup & Oil Consumption
problems.affectedModels: X166 GL 63 AMG, GLS 63 AMG (all M157.982).
problems.rootCause: The direct‑injection AMG V8 suffers from carbon buildup on intake valves — this leads to misfires and reduced performance. Oil consumption can also increase due to valve seal wear.
problems.recommendation: On a £20,000–£40,000 AMG GLS 63, a £800–£1,800 carbon cleaning service is cheap insurance — it protects the £9,000–£14,000 engine that sits at the heart of the car.

cta.label: Compare M157 engine prices for your GLS 63 →

problems.title: X166 AIRMATIC Suspension Wear
problems.affectedModels: All X166 variants, particularly high‑mileage examples.
problems.rootCause: The X166's AIRMATIC air suspension is the single most common non‑engine failure on this platform — air struts can leak, the compressor can fail, and valve block issues are documented.
problems.recommendation: This is the single most common non‑engine X166 failure — have the AIRMATIC system checked on any example over 80,000 miles.

replacementCosts.h2: X166 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are X166 SUV only. The X166 was a standalone full‑size SUV with no direct sibling body styles in this generation (unlike the smaller GLE, which had a coupé variant).
